name: Coastal Path from Playa del Puerto to Arnao and the Anchor Museum
name_original: Senda Costera de Playa del Puerto a Arnao y el Museo de Anclas
region_intro: Hiking around Castrillón, a municipality on the central coast of Asturias, Spain, offers diverse natural landscapes. The region features rugged coastlines with numerous beaches, coves, and cliffs, alongside a green interior with fertile valleys and rivers. Elevations increase towards the southwest, reaching the Alto del Prado del Marqués at 438 meters. Castrillón is also part of the Northern Route of the Camino de Santiago, providing established walking paths. Best hiking trails around Castrillón The most popular hiking route is Coastal Path from Playa del Puerto to Arnao and the Anchor Museum, a 6.5 miles (10.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 56 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows the coastline, passing through Arnao and its historical museum. Another top favourite among local hikers is Monument to Philippe Cousteau – Sails and Anchors Sculpture loop from Salinas, a moderate 6.1 miles (9.8 km) path. This loop from Salinas explores coastal areas and notable sculptures. Local hikers also love the Playa de Salinas – Sails and Anchors Sculpture loop from Salinas, a 4.3 miles (7.0 km) trail leading through coastal dunes and beaches, often completed in about 1 hour 48 minutes. Hiking around Castrillón is defined by its rugged coastlines, sandy beaches, and green interior valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy coastal walks to more challenging routes. The routes in Castrillón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 700 hikers have used komoot to explore Castrillón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Castrillón?
A: Castrillón offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 50 trails recorded on komoot. These routes cater to different preferences, from easy coastal strolls to more challenging inland explorations.

Q: Are there easy hiking options for beginners in Castrillón?
A: Yes, Castrillón has plenty of easy hiking options, with 23 routes classified as easy. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40318346" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de Salinas – Sails and Anchors Sculpture loop from Salinas&lt;/a&gt;, which is 4.3 miles (7.0 km) and takes about 1 hour 48 minutes, leading through coastal dunes and beaches.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Castrillón?
A: Many of Castrillón's trails are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6972933" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to Philippe Cousteau – Sails and Anchors Sculpture loop from Salinas&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate loop that explores coastal areas.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect on Castrillón's hiking trails?
A: Castrillón's hiking trails offer a diverse range of landscapes. You'll find rugged coastlines with numerous beaches, coves, and dramatic cliffs, as well as a green interior featuring fertile valleys, rivers like Fontaniella, Fureria, and Raíces, and increasing elevations towards the southwest, reaching the Alto del Prado del Marqués at 438 meters.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions to see while hiking in Castrillón?
A: Yes, Castrillón blends natural beauty with historical interest. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1048425389" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coastal Path from Playa del Puerto to Arnao and the Anchor Museum&lt;/a&gt; passes through Arnao, home to the Arnao Mine Museum, Spain's first documented underwater coal mine. Other nearby attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353340" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de San Martín&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353344" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Selgas Palace and the Jesús Nazareno Church‑Pantheon&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is Castrillón suitable for dog-friendly walks?
A: While specific dog-friendly regulations can vary by beach or protected area, the general nature of Castrillón's coastal and inland trails makes it a good region for walking with dogs. Always ensure your dog is under control and check local signage for any restrictions, especially on beaches during peak seasons.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking trails in Castrillón?
A: Many of the easier coastal walks and trails through the green interior are suitable for families. The diverse landscapes, including beaches like Salinas and Santa María del Mar, offer engaging environments for children. Look for routes classified as 'easy' on komoot for the most family-friendly options.

Q: What are the options for coastal walks in Castrillón?
A: Castrillón is renowned for its stunning coastal paths. You can explore numerous beaches such as Bayas, Deva, Salinas, Arnao, and Santa María del Mar. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1048425389" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coastal Path from Playa del Puerto to Arnao and the Anchor Museum&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering picturesque views and a moderate challenge.

Q: Are there long-distance hiking opportunities, like the Camino de Santiago, in Castrillón?
A: Yes, Castrillón is part of the 'Northern Route' of the Camino de Santiago, also known as the 'Route of Saint James'. This means you can find established walking paths that are part of this historic pilgrimage route, offering opportunities for longer walks and a unique cultural experience.

Q: Is public transport available to reach hiking trailheads in Castrillón?
A: Castrillón is a well-connected municipality in Asturias. While specific bus routes to every trailhead may vary, public transport options, including local buses, generally serve the main towns and coastal areas, making it possible to access many starting points for hikes. It's advisable to check local bus schedules for specific routes.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trails in Castrillón?
A: Many popular trailheads, especially those near beaches and towns like Salinas, Arnao, and Santa María del Mar, offer designated parking areas. For example, starting points for routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6971591" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa María del Mar Beach – Arnao Mine Museum loop from Salinas&lt;/a&gt; typically have parking facilities nearby.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Castrillón?
A: Castrillón benefits from a mild, oceanic climate, making hiking enjoyable for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and lush scenery, ideal for exploring both coastal and inland trails. Summer is also popular, especially for coastal walks combined with beach visits, though it can be busier. Winter hikes are possible, but be prepared for cooler, potentially wetter weather.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy the most about hiking in Castrillón?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 40 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the stunning coastal scenery, the variety of trails from easy beach walks to more challenging routes, and the opportunity to combine nature with cultural sites like the Arnao Mine Museum.
tips: The museum is very beautiful and interesting. The exhibition is not very big but the view of the old mine is worth it. For a guided tour it is essential to book in advance.

A monumental outdoor complex located at the end of Salinas Beach. It shows a collection of ship anchors, although the highlight is the spectacular views of the Cantabrian Sea. Free entrance. Essential visit.
A magnificent viewpoint that rises above the sea. A suspension bridge allows us to access the viewpoint, from which you can contemplate a fabulous panoramic view of Salinas and its surroundings.

Located inside the beautiful Museo de las Anclas (open-air museum). In memory of the late French oceanographer. Before its installation, it was exhibited in the Picasso room of the Unesco headquarters in Paris.

This tunnel has connected Salinas and Arnao since the mid-19th century. It was built for the railway that took the coal from the Arnao mine to the port of Avilés. After the closure of the mine, trucks from Asturiana de Zinc and the urban bus continue to pass through it daily.

waypoints.name: View of the coast from the entrance to the Arnao Tunnel
